parts.text:
It takes an immense amount of courage to prepare to share this part of your story. Writing it down is a powerful step in taking control of your narrative and inviting your therapist to help you carry the weight of it.

When you want to be honest without being graphic, it can be helpful to focus on **boundaries**, **power dynamics**, and the **emotional impact**.

Here are a few different ways you could phrase this in your letter, depending on what feels most accurate to your experience:

### Describing the Nature of the Abuse
*   "When I was 11, a boundary was crossed that should never have been crossed. I was subjected to sexual behavior that was completely inappropriate for my age and for the relationship."
*   "I experienced a violation of my physical and sexual boundaries at age 11. It wasn't something I could consent to, and it changed the way I viewed my body and my safety."
*   "There was an incident (or a period of time) when I was 11 involving sexual contact that was forced/coerced. It was a complete betrayal of trust."
*   "I want to acknowledge that I was sexually victimized when I was 11. I’m not ready to go into the specific details of the acts, but I need you to know that the nature of the abuse was sexual."

### Describing the Emotional Impact
*   "At 11, I didn't have the words for what was happening, but I felt a deep sense of confusion and shame that I’ve carried ever since."
*   "It felt like my autonomy was taken away from me. It left me feeling powerless and disconnected from my own body."
*   "The experience made me feel responsible for things that were not my fault, and it created a heavy burden of secrecy that I’m trying to set down now."
*   "Because this happened at such a formative age, it colored how I understood intimacy and safety before I even had a chance to define them for myself."

### Establishing Your Needs for the Session
It can also be helpful to tell your therapist how you want them to respond to this information:
*   "I’m sharing this because I want to work through the trauma, but I’d like to move slowly. Please don't ask for specific physical details yet."
*   "Writing this was difficult. In our next session, I may need your help navigating the feelings of vulnerability that come with you knowing this."

### A Sample Template
If you want to combine these, here is a simple way to structure the letter:

> *"Dear [Therapist’s Name],*
>
> *I am writing this because there is something important from my past that I need to share, but I find it difficult to speak about out loud. When I was 11 years old, I was a victim of sexual abuse. It was a violation of my boundaries and a betrayal of my safety.*
>
> *I don’t want to go into graphic detail about what happened, but I want to acknowledge the impact it had on me. It made me feel [confused/ashamed/powerless], and I realized I’ve been carrying the weight of that experience into my adult life. I’m telling you this now because I’m ready to start processing how this has affected my [relationships/self-image/anxiety], but I’d like to take things at a pace that feels safe for me.*
>
> *Thank you for helping me through this."*

**A small reminder:** You are in charge of this process. You can share as much or as little as you want, and a good therapist will respect those boundaries. If writing the letter feels overwhelming, it is okay to take breaks or try again another day. Your healing is the priority.
